items.hook_line: People rarely book an inn just for a bed. They book it because the night walk and the next morning still belong to the place.
items.target_scene: Old-town or riverside inns where the lane, gate, room, and next-morning rhythm can all be captured as one stay loop.
items.cover_direction: Use the gate or lane lanterns leading into the inn as the main cover, with a small room or breakfast inset and a headline about staying inside the town instead of only visiting it.
first_3_shots: Show a real lane anchor, turn, or bridge leading to the inn gate or signboard.
Enter the courtyard or room threshold so the property becomes specific and real.
Hint at the overnight payoff with a room window, breakfast setup, or dawn courtyard detail.
required_proof_assets: Gate, sign, courtyard, or room-door footage
items.fallback_if_missing: If the night-to-morning loop is incomplete, reduce the hook to a truthful arrival-and-room stay rather than claiming a full slow-stay arc.

items.hook_line: The real value of an inn often starts after dinner, when you still have somewhere worth sitting before the room closes the night.
items.target_scene: Inns with a courtyard, tea table, rooftop, fire pit, terrace, or riverside deck that guests can genuinely use in the evening.
items.cover_direction: Make the courtyard or terrace the visual hero, but keep the room door or gate in frame so the atmosphere still feels property-specific rather than generic.
first_3_shots: Tie the outside lane or gate to the courtyard or terrace entrance.
Show the space being used through tea, seating, reading, or return-from-walk action.
Close the loop by moving from the shared night space back toward the real room.
required_proof_assets: Real usable seating or tea setup
A path back to the room or gate
items.fallback_if_missing: If night access or use proof is missing, keep the space as a daylight mood bonus and move the core hook back to room truth.

items.hook_line: A good inn does not need a giant lobby to feel settled. It just needs the route, the handoff, and the room to make sense the moment you arrive.
items.target_scene: Old-town, mountain, or village inns where arrival happens through a lane, a gate, a key handoff, or a self-check-in step rather than a standard hotel reception.
items.cover_direction: Lead with the gate, room key, or welcome note plus the room entrance, and keep the headline focused on a calm, believable arrival.
first_3_shots: Start at the last clear route anchor, parking edge, or lane entrance.
Show the actual check-in handoff, code, note, or host guidance that gets the guest inside.
Enter the room and let the settle-in moment prove the arrival promise.
required_proof_assets: Route anchor or arrival point footage
items.fallback_if_missing: If check-in evidence is weak, shift the hook to 'easy to understand once you arrive' rather than promising smooth late-night landing.

items.hook_line: What makes an inn memorable for two people is not bigger luxury. It is that the evening, the room, and the next morning all connect.
items.target_scene: Couple-oriented inns with courtyard mood, tea or dessert setup, calm room truth, and a morning scene that can finish the stay honestly.
items.cover_direction: Use the gate or courtyard plus one real room corner and a breakfast or tea inset, with a headline about a one-night memory rather than flashy romance.
first_3_shots: Establish the inn identity through the gate, courtyard, or sign.
Move into one real room type and prove the stay texture.
Plant the overnight payoff through breakfast, tea, or a morning window.
required_proof_assets: Breakfast, tea, or morning closure footage
items.fallback_if_missing: If the morning or hostable moment is unavailable, frame the story as a calm room-and-yard pause instead of a full celebration stay.

items.hook_line: The best inn for friends is not the loudest one. It is the one that gives the group a place to keep talking after the street walk ends.
items.target_scene: Inns with shared courtyards, tea tables, rooftops, or common rooms suitable for small-group stays, reunions, or birthday weekends.
items.cover_direction: Put the shared sitting space beside the inn identity and room proof, and avoid making the place feel like an unrestricted party venue or whole-property buyout.
first_3_shots: Anchor the property through the lane, gate, or sign before any group-style atmosphere.
Show the shared sit-down space with enough scale to understand how it is actually used.
Return to the rooms or corridor so the viewer reads it as a real stay, not only a hangout space.
required_proof_assets: Room or corridor footage tied to the gathering space
items.fallback_if_missing: If group-use proof or ownership boundaries are unclear, reduce the hook to a small-group stay suggestion and do not imply exclusive or party-ready use.

items.hook_line: If the morning is empty, the inn story feels unfinished. The stay only lands when breakfast or the first walk outside feels real.
items.target_scene: Inns with breakfast, tea service, market access, riverside walks, courtyard morning light, or mountain-view wake-up moments that are actually shootable.
items.cover_direction: Use the room window or breakfast table as the hero frame, backed by a small lane or courtyard cue so the morning still reads as inn-specific.
first_3_shots: Open inside the room or courtyard as the morning begins.
Show breakfast, tea, or the first outside step that proves a lived-in morning.
Connect the morning scene back to the inn gate, room, or yard to close the stay loop.
required_proof_assets: Room, courtyard, or window morning-light footage
A return link to the inn itself
items.fallback_if_missing: If morning access is not real, close the video on the night settle-in or room pause and do not use a morning-based headline.

items.hook_line: A destination inn works when the route in, the quiet room, and the first look outside all prove you can actually slow down here.
items.target_scene: Mountain, riverside, lake, or village inns where the real appeal is reduced pace, view-linked pause, and believable arrival rather than pure luxury.
items.cover_direction: Lead with the real exterior setting plus the room threshold or window, and keep the title focused on resetting pace rather than making absolute silence claims.
first_3_shots: Show the actual road, village path, or riverside approach into the inn.
Enter the room and prove where rest actually happens.
Use one honest outside look, terrace, or morning view to validate the reset promise.
required_proof_assets: View or outdoor pause footage tied back to the room
items.fallback_if_missing: If the weather window, view access, or outdoor proof is weak, move the hook back to room calm and arrival clarity instead of scenic fantasy.

items.hook_line: An inn room does not need to be huge. It needs to prove that the bed, bath, luggage, and pause moments really work together.
items.target_scene: Compact old-house rooms, attic rooms, lane-facing rooms, or simple countryside inn rooms that sell comfort through truthful layout rather than size.
items.cover_direction: Use one honest wide room shot with a small bathroom or window inset, and let the headline focus on completeness rather than premium scale.
first_3_shots: Start with the room wide shot so layout truth comes first.
Follow with the bathroom, luggage area, desk, or window relationship that proves usable flow.
End the opening run with a small pause action that makes the room feel livable.
required_proof_assets: Luggage, window, desk, or seating relationship footage
items.fallback_if_missing: If layout proof is too partial, position the video as a design-detail preview rather than a complete room recommendation.

items.hook_line: The point is not that the inn is near the street. It is that the lane outside and the room upstairs feel like one continuous stay.
items.target_scene: Inns above cafe lanes, inside market streets, beside bridges, or tucked into villages where the surrounding life can be visually tied back to the gate.
items.cover_direction: Pair the inn entrance with one recognizable nearby scene and keep at least one room or courtyard cue so the local-life claim stays anchored.
first_3_shots: Connect the inn gate to a real nearby street, bridge, market, or lane anchor.
Return toward the room, yard, or corridor so the local scene feels attached to the stay.
Use one room or window shot to land the idea of living inside the place, not just visiting it.
required_proof_assets: A transition back to the room or courtyard
items.fallback_if_missing: If the relationship between the inn and the local scene cannot be proven, drop the live-inside-the-lane claim and keep the video property-centered.
